Laid Off 2 years ago - I have 5500$ to start a business/startup . What can I do?

comments.body:
Don’t spend the money. 
I am working on my startup - let’s have a chat and see if there’s alignment.
DM.
100% Amazon FBA

First step is to have a plan, there are no get rich quick schemes.

You can attempt Amazon and dropshipping etc but these things are mega competitive and you’ll likely run yourself down to zero.

5500 is not much to start a business. I’d recommend finding work whilst you prepare and plan then once you’re ready you’ll know what you need to do.
It doesn't sound like you are in the best place to fund a startup at the moment. You should probably find a stable job or start freelancing if you can find clients.
Let's connect or DM me we will discuss don't lose hope ..
Services

I know you said no to “get a job”, but what about certification courses? If you’re in project management, do you do SCRUM? That’s a weekend-long course that has gotten two of my coworkers hired within the last year.

Use that moola to start a service based business. No need for fancy tech or a big upfront investment. Think cleaning services, digital marketing, or even freelance project management. Low overhead, quick turnaround, and you can start generating income fast. Build a basic website, get some business cards, and hustle for your first clients. Skip the job hunt, create your own opportunity. You're a product manager, so you know how to manage and deliver.... now just do it for yourself!!!!
[deleted]

Learn a technical skill and offer services using that skill - Design, Marketing, Sales etc. Build portfolio by offering it for free. Do it fast. 

PMs are general managers and lack hard skills. In tough markets, employers appreciate hard skills more than soft skills.
If you have great idea, I could be your tech co-founder. Dm me if you have great idea, I wouldn't want to spend time on bad idea tho

I wouldnt build a start up with the hope of generating income fast, it shouldn't be the first goal (it will wreck your mental health, I've been there). I'd recommend you stabilize your personal income first, so that you are mentally in the right place, I've done it this way:

- List 10 small businesses you would love to work with, it can be anything. (2 hrs)

- Draft a one-pager of who you are, and what you can help with with your skillset. (1 hour: no more, otherwise you will spend days on this ahah)

- Reach out to founders and offer a no-brainer offer for less than 1000$ to help them with one challenging tech/product/automation thing. (3 hours)

- Get your foot in the door with this "trial mission". Once you establish a relationship, improve your game so that you can sell your time for 300 to 500$/day worked. 2days worked per week\*400$= 3200/month. 

- Then you can try to land another client with testimonials from the first one, or increase your number of days with the same one, or increase your daily rate. 

The rest of your week, build your start up (if you still want to). You can even use your clients interactions, to spot problems and build a start up out of that.

And yesss, skip the job hunt, create your own opportunities, there are TONS out there :)
Hi, are you open for sales job in medical service industry? If so please DM
Chatgpt innit

House cleaning startup. Go to local fb groups, reddit groups, IG and tezt everyone non-stop offering to clean their house



Message for 4hrs every day. You will 100% find clients

digital marketing is not a business to enter when you have no money. I know owner of Pixel Prodigies, he saved up 30K before starting digital marketing business and had clientelle lined up already before quitting his job and years of skillset.

  
If no money best businesses to start with no skill are cleaning, dogwalking, babysitting, moving business, etc.

  
Alternatively if he needs quick money he can Uber
I love how it is written like an instagram post. Chatgpt not getting how reddit works.

Hey there so I've been a Product Manager and I'll share some advice with you.

Starting a business is no different then applying most of the skills of product... You need to identify your customer or market problem and put a solution together that solves that problem. 

But first... Put alot of effort into finding another job. Its going to be hard but if you are looking for ROI it will take a while to grow any business to the size where it covers a PM salary.

That being said... Services are where to look. I started a hot tub service company for like $500. I had a lawyer write up a contract, I got a web site going that I created on WordPress, and setup a stripe account to collect payments. Made $1500 my first month and it was great. The company has 2 employees now and more or less runs it's self. 

My wife and I also started a construction company. I used my PM skills to market and build our customer pipeline. I also got licensed to do excavation. 

Now that I'm going back to work as a PM we still have these two companies running.

The best you could do is outsource to d-tier developers in a very very low income country and get something either very simple or pretty unpolished and half working.


Maybe you can provide very good specs to get what you want developed if your a really good PM, but do you know the market? To found a startup you also need to be a good sales person.


But where do you live? Your own cost of living will matter a lot, and whether you can speak the first language of cheap developers, plus your own local market. 


There's no way you could break into a mainstream market so you'd need to find a niche market, and you would want to do the market research and find interested clients up front before you even build it.


Also, these projects never finish on budget or on time so if this is your emergency fund I don't see how you can build a business immediately with no safety net, wiggle room, or backup plan.


If you're looking for people to give you an idea then you're kidding yourself. If someone has a surefire idea that can make a profit right off the bat with barely any funding and can be developed incredibly quickly then they'd do it themselves.
Sorry 5500 is not going to cut it.

This is one time that I agree with the crowd.

You could maybe take part of that the money to develop a skill or get a quality certification.

Then get a job.
Whatever you do should be within $500-$1000 only coz the rest will go into iterations and buffer to make it actually work
I have a product, need a partner. Hit me up. 40/60.
Freelance to a startup. Think about AI moviemaking

Declaration is the antithesis of good business and life decisions. You need a job bro, you don't just start a business and have income when you have no plan. You will have to take any job you can, to have some foundation to build from and not attempt desperate acts.
What skills do you have? Can you start a dev team?

Don't do this. The chances of a well funded business venture succeeding at all ( typically doesn't turn a profit for years) is in the single digits. The chance of success for something with only $5500, is a very small percent of that single digit. The chance of success for something where a person had so little ambition that they haven't worked in 2 years is a small percent of that small percent of that small percent. The chance of success for something where that person can't even find a job and thinks that isn't an option is a small percent of that small percent of that.... well, you get the idea.

Anyone encouraging you to spend that last $5500 on starting a business either has no idea what they are talking about, or wants to see you crash and burn.

Starting a business is in no way shape or form, something you do to make immediate money to live off. You literally have a better chance at the casino putting it all on black. Seriously, it's not even about losing the money. Losing it fast would probably be a much better outcome than the he'll you have in front of you if you were to start down the entrepreneur road now with where you are today. Then with that money gone, you can get back on the real path you need to be on. Solving whatever reason you can't even get a job. Start there as soon as possible.

I'm going to give you serious advice because I am also where you are (well, 80% of the same condition). I'm a technical expert in my field, and it's been impossible for me to land a steady day job gig for two years. The responses I get are that either I'm "too senior" or that my "skills aren't where we'd like them to be." I was homeless before I started my career, and I am willing to kill myself to keep my family from suffering what I endured. In my moonlighting hours, I kept working as much as I could on my startup *because it gave me hope*. But my growing burnout and anxiety became the main impediment. Hell, I even whipped up a SBIR proposal at the end of last year that was rejected -- I've since re-reading my proposal that my cofounders "helped" me to do (really, they were offering emotional support for me, rather than technical support because they also have their own crises to manage). And OOF! it reeks of desparation, trying to pitch three different things that my product will someday be able to do, and just lacking any real focus. Absolutely not my best work, especially with my track record in proposal wins from my earlier career days.

Recently, I took a look at my IRA. It's a retirement account I set up through Fidelity *way back*, about a decade ago. I got fired from a gig then for championing that everyone in the org should work together and that the top manager shouldn't split teams up based on employment status (yea, I should have known better, but the *Tism*^(TM) is strong in me!). Anyway, I just kept rolling all of the 401k accounts I had from everywhere I worked into it. This year, it seemed to do amazingly well. And I got some advice on considering using it. And so, my family took a little bit of money out of it instead of asking for yet another interest-free loan from my brother.

I admit, I got lucky and now I'm in the position I'm in. And luck is not universally useful, let alone controllable & repeatable. I don't know what your living expenses are, but $5.5k USD is about *half* of what my family needs me to contribute for us to survive month-to-month. And, well, we don't live extravagantly: It's my wife and our three fur-babies living in a suburban 100yr old house whose mortgage cost per month is about 1/3 of the average rent in the area. So it's from this perspective I'm going to give you advice:

***Don't start a business when you're fighting for survival. Otherwise you will surely kill yourself before it succeeds. And then what was the point of your actions?***

If I were you, I'd take that $5.5k and give yourself a small break. Maybe a week or so, depending on your budget. Decompress, meet up with your real-world friends, take a psychological vacation and reconnect with "simple" things you've probably foregone in your fight for survival. You're not giving up: You are recovering from the same burnout I'm facing. And remind yourself that every day! But find a way to maximize your ability to let go of your stressors for a little bit so that you can look at your life objectively.

Speaking for myself, I do have a little bit of "work" to do that isn't startup stuff. But it's giving me a pathway so that I can return to building the business I really want to create. It's what allowed me to self-critique my funding proposal objectively.

I had lunch with my entrepreneurial mentor yesterday. And it hit me hard when he told me that I looked better than I had in well over a year. *And he is jealous* (well, in a healthy way). So that's my advice: Take care of your self first. If you do not, you are guaranteed to fail, lose yourself, or both.

Talk to a ton of people in a space you care about, regarding a handful of problems that you think are worth solving. See the patterns, identify something that a lot of people deeply care about.  
Validate solutions to those problems, talk to another ton of relevant people and create a prototype or wireframe of a productized version of this solution \[consider using free tools - moqups, marvel, figma, etc\]

show it to more people, validate and iterate.  
Then you have a solid product idea.  
And you haven't spent a penny yet.

Build an MVP that will get you your initial 100-200 users.  
That will lead to some converting into paying users and you can get insights from them, double down on what works and grow it.

Easier said than done.

But $5.5k is enough to get things built till the MVP stage - especially with nocode.  
As long as you don't spend money on things that don't add value to you.

Building the MVP could be somewhere you spend around 3-4k  
And 1k could be spent on actually getting initial traction on your MVP once you have iterated and received your initial few paying users.

Hang on to some of that cash for running costs too.  
But you can solopreneur this thing in a few months.  
I've seen people doing that.
Buy bounce house and tents for parties.

agree 100%
After all, building a startup is a bet, like buying a stock. You should only invest (time, money, etc.) that you can afford to lose.
“I understand technical stuff” doesn’t inspire a lot of confidence

5500 is not enough and it takes to much time to see the monthly income you'll need to live off of.

Product managers are some of the most hireable positions within tech and startups. I would be suprised if you couldn't find something somewhere.

If you can't find a job as a product manager then do something else in the meantime like managing the front of house in a restaurant or even waiting tables. Just until you can replace your PM role. 

In order for startups to succeed it really helps to be passionate about the problem you are trying to solve. Your looking at it purely from the perspective of necessity and money and that isn't enough, especially during the times when there is no income and you need that grit to keep going despite not knowing whether your product is good or not etc.

Get a job, any job to pay the bills. Try to replace your PM role in the meantime. Don't like the jobs available in your area, move. Apply for global remote jobs. If you NEED to start a business, make it a cashflow business such as landscaping or painting. Cheap to start and operate and can provide immediate monthly income.

I don't think that is the main problem. You can bootstrap a business with even less than that. The main problem is trying to find a startup/business that is going to generate a good and stable income from pretty much day 1.
If u r going back to work means the company u started not doing well...

Nope doesn't mean that at all. My wife and I are trying to have kids and the reliable income/ insurance is easier to get with me having a full time job. 

Also we want to continue buying real estate/ refinance which is much easier to do with high W2 income.

It’s not an easy or quick thing. You can build a site and client list (time), you can freelance (1099), or you can find a short term gig to augment finances (e.g. would you like fries with that). 

When it comes to survival, pride will kill you.

As someone who has worked 3 part time shit jobs just to afford to live in the back of a car once upon a time… buckle down and get comfortable being uncomfortable. 

Or sacrifice the future you want for immediate desires and comfort.

Pipe dream time… If you start a site it will cost you $20~ per month for a domain and a Google workspace account basic site. $40 for DocuSign. $12~ Midjourney. Stripe for payment processing. A handful of free tools and some time on YouTube and your 5500 gives you 76 months of business platform. 

If you don’t need it to eat or for a place to live.

If you’re good at your job and with people you can have a business, licensing, and clients in 1 week revenue depends on your pricing and model.

Honestly though… The time to start was 2 years ago, when you got laid off. 

It’s been two years and you aren’t cash flowing yet… I don’t know that I’d take those odds. 

Please take this as the kindness it’s intended to be and use it to take inventory of your mindset, circumstances, and options. Then decide if starting out as a small business is the right call. 

Look at the statistics for small business failure/closure. Look at the amount of hours you will need to put in. 

If you don’t love your work or struggle to maintain a normal employer it’s not something I’d recommend to most people.

I genuinely wish you well, I hope you not only succeed, but thrive. Good luck.

comments.body:
New invite as of November 7!

👋 If you're looking to take control of your personal finances, I’ve been using [Monarch Money](https://www.monarchmoney.com/referral/bbiy1zuthv) and it's been a game-changer. Monarch Money is an all-in-one financial planning tool that lets you:

* Track your spending and budgeting
* Set savings and investment goals
* Link all your accounts for easy tracking

It’s helped me get a clear picture of my financial goals. And, with my [invite](https://www.monarchmoney.com/referral/bbiy1zuthv), you’ll get an **extended 30-day free trial** instead of the standard two weeks. No credit card required to start – just give it a try and see what you think!  Let me know if you have any questions! 😊
